skill.name: prompt-caching
skill.description: Guides LLM prompt caching strategy, response caching, cache invalidation, and Cache Augmented Generation design.
skill.summary: Plan cost-aware prompt and response caching for LLM applications.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported Ruby or shell backtick execution on SKILL.md line 61, but the line contains inline Markdown references to related skills. The weak cryptographic algorithm alert on SKILL.md line 3 appears to be a substring match in descriptive text, with no code, commands, network access, secrets, or prompt injection found.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Backticks
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged Ruby or shell backtick execution at SKILL.md line 61. The line only lists related skill names inside Markdown inline code formatting, so there is no executable command path.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged backticks are Markdown formatting around related skill names. No shell, Ruby, script block, or command invocation is present.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Pattern
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged a weak cryptographic algorithm at SKILL.md line 3. The line is a skill description about caching prompts and does not contain cryptographic code or algorithm selection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged location is descriptive frontmatter text. I found no cryptographic implementation, hash function call, or security-sensitive algorithm usage.

content.value_statement: LLM applications can waste tokens and latency when repeated prompt context is not cached. This skill helps teams choose prompt caching, response caching, and CAG patterns with clear invalidation rules.
actual_capabilities: Explains when to use native prompt prefix caching for repeated context.
Compares prompt caching, response caching, KV caching, and Cache Augmented Generation.
Identifies cache invalidation risks for changing data and stale responses.
Recommends prompt structure changes that improve cache hit rates.
Highlights anti-patterns such as high-temperature response caching and caching everything.
limitations: Does not implement a cache store, SDK wrapper, or production service.
Does not benchmark token savings or latency in a live application.
Does not provide vendor-specific API code beyond conceptual guidance.
Requires users to supply their prompt structure and application constraints.

use_cases.title: Reduce Repeated Context Cost
use_cases.description: Design prompts so stable instructions and documents can be cached across many LLM calls.

use_cases.title: Plan Response Cache Rules
use_cases.description: Decide which requests can reuse full responses and which require fresh generation.

use_cases.title: Evaluate CAG Versus RAG
use_cases.description: Compare preloaded document context with retrieval-based generation for narrow knowledge tasks.

prompt_templates.title: Identify Cache Opportunities
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of an existing LLM workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my LLM workflow and identify which prompt parts are stable, which parts vary, and where prompt caching could reduce cost.

prompt_templates.title: Design Response Caching
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a response caching plan for these request types. Include cache keys, TTLs, invalidation triggers, and cases that must bypass the cache.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Caching Patterns
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare prompt caching, response caching, KV caching, and Cache Augmented Generation for this application. Recommend one primary approach and explain the tradeoffs.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Cache Reliability
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this LLM caching strategy for stale data, privacy exposure, hit-rate instability, latency spikes, and cost leakage. Propose concrete mitigations.

output_examples.input: A support assistant repeats the same policy manual in every request.
output_examples.output: Recommendation: place the policy manual in the stable prompt prefix and keep user questions after it. Track cache hit rate and update the prefix only when policies change.

output_examples.input: A chat app wants to cache answers while using high temperature.
output_examples.output: Recommendation: avoid full response caching for creative replies. Cache stable context instead, or lower temperature for deterministic FAQ-style requests.

output_examples.input: A team is choosing between CAG and RAG for a small document set.
output_examples.output: Recommendation: use CAG when the document set is small, stable, and frequently reused. Use RAG when documents are large, dynamic, or need precise retrieval.

best_practices: Place stable instructions, schemas, and documents before volatile user input.
Define invalidation rules before enabling response caching in production.
Measure cache hit rate, cost savings, stale response rate, and cache miss latency.
anti_patterns: Caching high-temperature responses as if they are deterministic.
Changing prompt prefixes on every request and expecting cache hits.
Caching sensitive or user-specific content without isolation rules.

faq.question: What problem does this skill solve?
faq.answer: It helps design LLM caching strategies that reduce repeated token cost and avoid stale or unsafe cached outputs.

faq.question: Does it write production cache code?
faq.answer: No. It provides architecture guidance, review prompts, and design checks for caching decisions.

faq.question: When should I use prompt caching instead of response caching?
faq.answer: Use prompt caching when the context is repeated but user requests still need fresh generation.

faq.question: When is response caching appropriate?
faq.answer: Use response caching for deterministic requests where the same input should return the same answer.

faq.question: What is Cache Augmented Generation?
faq.answer: CAG preloads relevant documents into context so the model can answer without a separate retrieval step.

faq.question: Is this skill safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes. The reviewed file contains guidance text only, and the static security alerts were false positives.
